description: The following recommendations should be considered while configuring the IP addresses for primary and backup controllers: While configuring a primary-controller ip <ipv4-address>, it is recommended to use the IP address of a standalone controller or an IP address of a controller in the primary cluster. Similarly, while configuring a backup-controller ip <ipv4-address>, it is recommended to use the IP address of a standalone controller or an IP address of a controller in the backup cluster. Configuring both primary or backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster is not supported.

messages.content: For 6300_6300L_6400 AOS-CX 10.13.1130, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - The following recommendations should be considered while configuring the IP addresses for primary and backup controllers: While configuring a primary-controller ip <ipv4-address>, it is recommended to use the IP address of a standalone controller or an IP address of a controller in the primary cluster. Similarly, while configuring a backup-controller ip <ipv4-address>, it is recommended to use the IP address of a standalone controller or an IP address of a controller in the backup cluster. Configuring both primary or backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster is not supported.

description: User-Based Tunneling (UBT) and DHCPv4/v6 snooping are mutually exclusive features in AOS-CX. When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. If the port is a member of multiple VLANs, then the VLAN-level configuration for IP source lockdown will not be applied. Client IP tracker can track the IP address until the IP source lockdown is applied. Once the lockdown is in effect, the IP address will only be tracked if there is a valid entry in the binding table. However, if an IP address is already being tracked, it will remain active through probes, as probes utilize ARP packets, which are Layer 2 (L2) packets rather than Layer 3 (L3) packets.

When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. description: Symptom: Link drops might be observed when the DAC cables, S1J07A or S1J08A is used for the products, S0E91A and S0X44A. Scenario: This issue is observed after upgrading the switches from 10.13.1000 or a later 10.13.1xxx version using VSF ISSU. Workaround: The products, S0E91A and S0X44A must be rebooted after an upgrade. Upgrade either the entire stack or only the stand alone switches based on your deployment. For stack with a mix of S0E91A, S0X44A, and other 6300 switches, either reboot the entire stack or perform ISSU and then manually reboot only the S0E91A and S0X44A switches using the vsf member <1-10> reboot command.

For stack with a mix of S0E91A, S0X44A, and other 6300 switches, either reboot the entire stack or perform ISSU and then manually reboot only the S0E91A and S0X44A switches using the vsf member <1-10> reboot command.

description: The switch does not support double encapsulation. A packet can be encapsulated with either L2GRE (UBT) or VXLAN, but not both. The network administrator should decide the tunneling type to be used, and then plan the configuration accordingly.

messages.content: For 6300_6300L_6400 AOS-CX 10.13.1130, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?

description: In the event of license issues when onboarding a DUT to primary or backup mobility conductor, the DUT will not try to bootstrap to another mobility conductor where a license is available. For example, if the primary mobility conductor does not have a license to onboard the DUT but the backup mobility conductor does have adequate licenses, if both mobility conductors are reachable then UBT will be down, and the DUT will not attempt to bootstrap to the backup controller. However, if the primary mobility conductor is not reachable, the DUT gets tunneled to the standby/backup mobility conductor. Once the primary mobility conductor is reachable by the DUT once again, the DUT will not automatically bootstrap back to the primary. Network administrators should manually disable and enable UBT on the DUT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary mobility conductor.

messages.content: For 6300_6300L_6400 AOS-CX 10.13.1130, what additional caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - In the event of license issues when onboarding a DUT to primary or backup mobility conductor, the DUT will not try to bootstrap to another mobility conductor where a license is available. However, if the primary mobility conductor is not reachable, the DUT gets tunneled to the standby/backup mobility conductor. Once the primary mobility conductor is reachable by the DUT once again, the DUT will not automatically bootstrap back to the primary. Network administrators should manually disable and enable UBT on the DUT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary mobility conductor.

description: When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

messages.content: For 6300_6300L_6400 AOS-CX 10.13.1130, what caveat is documented for Central?

This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

description: Symptom: 802.1X re-authentication interval for authenticated clients does not match the configured value, with re-authentication occurring later than expected. Scenario: This issue occurs when using 802.1X authentication with re-authentication enabled. The system may delay the re-authentication process by several minutes beyond the configured re-authentication period. The issue does not affect the authentication status of clients, only the timing of re-authentication.

description: Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in "device-mode" instead of "client-mode":{color:#000000}interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device{color}

description: Symptom: Missing ARP entries showing as "incomplete" in the ARP table, causing connectivity issues in remote VTEP. Scenario: This issue may occur in an EVPN VXLAN environment with VSX pairs when a VIP (Virtual IP) moves from one node to another node, MAC address aging occurs or after executing "clear mac-address" on the switch. Workaround: A temporary workaround is to add a static ARP entry for the affected IP addresses.

description: Symptom: Traffic drop is observed and BGP sessions go down when the ENCAP MAC SA programming points the wrong MAC address, VLAN, and port from the ECMP member group. Scenario: This issue can occur when the following conditions are met: GRE tunnels are configured. Tunnels are up after route resolution. The nexthop mac alone is changed for the same VLAN and port combination. The change in the nexthop mac alone leads to an issue in the programming of the ENCAP MAC SA and causes the traffic drop and BGP sessions to go down. Workaround: Shut/no shut the tunnel to delete the ENCAP MAC SA and re-add the correct nexthop mac, port, and VLAN. This makes the BGP session up with no traffic drop.

description: Symptom: Continuous system reboots occur on the affected switch. The system enters a reboot loop, preventing normal network operation and management access. Scenario: This issue occurs in a specific VSF environment when the VXLAN Tunnel Underlay routes are being resolved. Before the system could process the route resolution and program the tunnel, the commander went for a reboot. When the new commander came and when the system started processing the request, it programmed tunnel underlay with invalid information. The invalid configuration prevents the member from successfully joining the stack, triggering continuous reboots. Workaround: To resolve this issue, flap the underlay connections using the VXLAN shut and no shut commands. These events will clear the incorrect status information and should reprogram the tunnel properly.

description: Symptom: The standby stack interface shows an uptime that appears unchanged, not reflecting the link down/up cycle. Scenario: This issue occurs when performing a VSF switchover in a two-member stack. When the commander rejoins as standby, check the standby stack interface uptime using show interface <standby stack interface> on the new commander. The uptime remains nearly the same as before the switchover. However, with the fix, the interface shows a new uptime, indicating the link properly went down and came back up during the switchover.

description: Symptom: Traffic loss in an SVI with vsx active-forwarding enabled, when vsx active-forwarding is disabled/removed on a entirely different SVI. Scenario: In a configuration where vsx active-forwarding is enabled, a special HW resource must be programmed for the VSX peers to handle traffic destined to the active-gateway IP. The trigger for this programming is tied to the router MAC programming), and the ref counting for these router MACs would decrement early, removing the special HW resource and causing traffic loss to occur depending on which peer it lands on. Workaround: There are two possible workarounds: 1. Unconfigure and reconfigure vsx active-forwarding in the SVI. 2. Save the configuration and reboot the VSX peers
